content:
Egregio, così parrebbe, ora farò le prove del caso e poi relazionerò nel merito .

Da Google ...

Sì, un MacBook Pro è in grado di gestire chiavette USB formattate sia in NTFS che in exFAT, ma con differenze sostanziali nel supporto in lettura e scrittura.

exFAT (Scelta consigliata per Mac + Windows): Il Mac legge e scrive nativamente su chiavette formattate in exFAT. È il formato ideale per trasferire file di grandi dimensioni (> 4GB) tra macchine macOS e Windows.

NTFS (Solo lettura su Mac): Il Mac legge nativamente i file su chiavette NTFS, ma non può scriverci sopra (non puoi aggiungere, modificare o cancellare file) senza software di terze parti.

Per ora grazie per il suggerimento a presto

content:
Next, buon pomeriggio !

Allora ora sono attrezzato a dovere e ho provveduto a fare il backup della cartella "Thunderbird" dal Pc Win 11 Pro, poi sono andato a cercare le istruzioni per restorare su Mac, eccole:

Per trasferire Thunderbird da Windows 11 a Mac, la soluzione migliore è copiare l'intera cartella del profilo. Chiudi Thunderbird su PC, naviga in %appdata%, copia la cartella Thunderbird su chiavetta USB, installa Thunderbird su Mac, e incolla la cartella nel percorso ~/Library/Thunderbird/ su Mac. Ecco i passaggi dettagliati per una migrazione completa (email, impostazioni, password): 1. Operazioni su PC Windows 11 

   Chiudi completamente Thunderbird.    Premi i tasti Windows + R, digita %appdata% e premi Invio.    Individua la cartella Thunderbird (solitamente in Roaming\Thunderbird).    Copia l'intera cartella Thunderbird su un supporto di archiviazione esterno    (chiavetta USB o disco esterno).

2. Operazioni su MacBook Pro 

   Scarica e installa Thunderbird dal sito ufficiale sul Mac.    Chiudi Thunderbird immediatamente dopo l'installazione (non configurare alcun account).    Apri il Finder, seleziona "Vai" nella barra dei menu e scegli "Vai alla cartella...".    Digita ~/Library/Application Support/ e cerca una cartella chiamata Thunderbird.     Se non esiste, creala.    Copia la cartella Thunderbird salvata in precedenza dalla chiavetta USB e incollala    in ~/Library/Application Support/.        Nota: Se esiste già una cartella "Thunderbird" creata dall'installazione,     sostituiscila con quella proveniente da Windows.    Avvia Thunderbird. Tutte le email, gli account e le impostazioni dovrebbero essere    presenti.

Peccato che non funziona ! Forse sarò io a sbagliare nel fare qualcosa, ma ho eseguito scrupolosamente le istruzioni, però quando sul MacBook cerco di lanciare Thunderbird, questo si apre ma come si apre anche appena installato, ovvero offre la videata per inserire i dati del primo account di posta elettronica, il che ovviamente non pare combaciare con le istruzioni più sopra, mentre la copia dal Pc Win 11 pro si realizza pefettamente e così pure il trasferimento dalla chiavetta al MacBook, ma l'incolla sulla cartella esistente della nuova installazione Thunderbird sul Mac non va, e neppure se sempre seguendo suggerimento si cancella prima la cartella Thunderbird e poi si copia in "Library/Application support/" , nel senso che poi si riavvia il Mac e poi si prova a lanciare Thunderbird, questo restituisce la solita videata di partenza ovvero quando, come già più sopra evidenziato, propone la finestra per inserire il primo account di posta.

Se puoi aiutarmi, io eseguirò quanto richiesto alla lettera e vediamo se alla fine ne verrò fuori ... Grazie per l'attenzione sinora prestata F.

content:
I'm not sure if it's the only, or best, way but I use my mail host's caldav and carddav service for that.

First, check if your mail host provides caldav and cardav service. If they do, they will probably provide instructions for configuring thunderbird to access them.

Once that is done, thunderbird on both your machines will periodically synchronise, thereby sharing your events and contacts via the host's service. It also means you can access your calendar and contacts from most mobiles, even if they don't support thunderbird.

If your mail host doesn't provide the service, then you might be able to set up caldav and carddav on your shared network device but I suspect that would be a lot more complicated.

content:
I think that might be risk data corruption, especially if the two PCs both try to access thunderbird at the same time. A system intended for multiple users would ensure that if two people try to update the same database or variable at the same time, their changes happen sequentially or in a co-ordinated fashion, rather than both writing to the same record and one of them deleting the other's work. Thunderbird is designed for a single user, so it's unlikely to be implemented that way,

It could possibly work if the two users were "me, when I', at work" and "me, when I'm at home" so that the twothunderbirds never get used at the same time. But even then, there's a danger that you accidentally leave thunderbird open when you go home and possibly then thunderbird might not let you back in at home.

content:
I can describe how my system works.

It's based around thunderbird, which I have used since I registered my own domain name in 2013 but I used thunderbird with other email addresses for many years before that.

It's not essential to register your own domain name. But owning an email address in the form *.myChosenDomain.org.uk has some advantages over an email address like onefixedname@somespywarecompany.com:

  • it's a wildcard address so I can instantly create additional addresses like info@myChosenDomain.org.uk or 2026party@my..... if I want a separate mailbox for them.
  • the domain is owned by me, not by some spyware company, so if the company hosting my mail service increases the price, becomes unreliable or even goes out of business, I can move to another mail host without having to tell everybody a new email address.

I'm using netcetera as my domain registrar, but you can use any reliable registrar that provides a name-only service at a reasonable price.

Nevertheless, you can stick with the email address provided by your existing mail host if you wish -- provided it has well documented support for SMTP and IMAP so that thunderbird can send and receive mail through it.

I have a collection of old laptops, some of which occasionally run thunderbird. They each have separate thunderbird profiles, sending and receiving mail via my mail host's SMTP and IMAP service.

My android (lineageos) phone previously used the built in mail reader but a few years ago I replaced it with k9 (which is now operating as part of mozilla -- see https://k9mail.app/2023/12/28/When-will-Thunderbird-for-Android-be-released). This works similar to thunderbird, sending and receiving mail via my mail host's SMTP and IMAP services.

My current mail host is mailbox.org. I've only used this for a few months, when my previous mail host for many years "enhanced" its mail software and became unreliable. So far mailbox is working well.

My calendar has always been based in thunderbird, so when I started using mobiles it was logical to find a caldav service so the two could share data. My mail host at the time did not operate caldav so I created an account with GMX and used that until a month ago, when one of my three calendars refused to accept new entries from firefox. It was the biggest of my calendars and I think it might just have grown too big for GMX. Mailbox provides a caldav service, so I transferred the calendars across to it and that too seems to be working well. At the same time I transferred my contacts from GMX to mailbox.

In summary, everything is now either local instances ( thunderbird on windows, or k9 on lineageos/android) linked using mailbox.org SMTP/IMAP/CalDav/CardDav).

That said, setting up something like the above does involve a learning curve if you haven't done it before. My time over the next four weeks is going to be limited because of a special event that's coming up, so although I'll keep an eye on this thread it might be wise to do only initial tests until you're comfortable about it working as you need.
creator: harry.green

content:
Sounds like something is interfering and the most common culprit is an Anti-Virus program.

It may be scanning incoming mail before it allow Thunderbird access and/or scanning any file that gets opened in the profile before anything can get written to a file etc. It can cause all sorts of performance issues.

Compacting means Thunderbird creates a new mbox file eg: Inbox- it then opens the current Inbox mbox file and reads it - anything that does not have a 'marked as deleted' flag meaning anything previous either deleted or moved - gets copied to the new file. Then when completed, the original is deleted and the new Inbox replaces it. This is repeated for all your mbox files = folders as seen in Thunderbird.

When Thunderbird wants to compact all folders - it is advisable to do the following. Put Thunderbird in Offline mode, thus ensuring nothing is incoming, do not select any folder or email or create any emails - just wait and under no circumstance exit/close Thunderbird. The whole process should not take more than a few moments. If you are just doing a manual compact on one folder eg: Inbox, then it's likely to be a couple of seconds. So going offline should not be necessary.

What Anti-Virus are you using ? Advise is to access Anti-Virus and set up an exception to not scan your Thunderbird profile folder or anything it contains.

If you downloaded Thunderbird from the official website: https://www.thunderbird.net/en-US/thunderbird/all/

then the 'Thunderbird' profile folder to exclude from scans is here; C://Users/username/Appdata/Roaming/Thunderbird

content:
Emails that contain bad stuff tend to include them as attachments or links or remote content.

Downloading and opening an email in itself does not auto open any attachment nor select and open any link to a bad website.

Only you can click on an attachment to open it and no one should ever open an attachment just because they are curious. Only open attachments if you know they are from a reliable source. Some attachments can look like eg: a pdf but in reality they are not. I get plenty of bogus attempts to say eg: invoice ia attached when I know I've not ordered anything. Best advised action is delete the email and then compact folder to fully remove from files.

Thunderbird by default does not allow any Remote Content, that option can only change if you deliberately activate and allow it to occur.

  • Settings > Privacy & Security
  • Mail content
  • Advise is - Do not select this: 'Allow remote content in messages'

More info on Remote Content:

  • https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/remote-content-in-messages

AS for links which may be appear as text or look like a webpage link or be included in an image etc. Never click on a link which may appear as ok until you have checked if it is correct and expected. How to check a link ? Hover cursor over link (do Not click on it) and the real link is displayed in the bottom Status bar.

You are more likely to get a virus auto downloaded by accessing a bad webpage which hopefully your AV is checking as well.

I've had plenty of emails pretending to be from a legit company and the link in email that looks ok, when you hover over it, the real link is obviously very wrong.

Even if you want your emails scanning on download, you do not want your profile folders and files being scanned when Thunderbird wants to write to them or scanned during a normal scan.

Even if it finds something is wrong in a thunderbird profile, you never want it to perform an auto fix because no Anti-virus understands that an mbox file is likely to contain a lot of good wanted emails, it thinks there is only the one and a fix on the mbox file will probably delete everything in it. An mbox file in profile = a folder in Thunderbird UI.

Frequently, if AV finds something, the odds are you have already deleted that bad email, but not cleaned up the file, so AV is detecting a 'marked as deleted' email. Compacting the folder will remove it and sort out the problem in a manner that does not effect other emails in same file.

content:
Hello there



Sync related issues Nextcloud 32 is very recent. There may be a slight mismatch between the software, Thunderbird, and certain Linux or daily Thunderbird settings. Data interpretation acts differently. Go to Settings > General, scroll to the bottom, and click Config Editor. Search for calendar.view.visible or calendar.list.sortOrder. Warning: Do not change values unless instructed. Look for calendar.caldav.sched.enabled; setting this to true can sometimes resolve sync-related UI locks.

Is it a Bug? Yes, likely. There have been several

content:
1. check that all messages are downloaded. clicking file>offline>download is a suggested step
  2. then, click help>troubleshootinginformation
  3. scroll to 'profile folder' and click 'open folder'
  4. exit thunderbird
  5. you are now in the profile. the IMAP account is at imapmail\yourIMAPaccount>
  6. click to that folder and locate your message files. they will appear twice, such as inbox, inbox.msf, sent, sent.msf, etc.
  7. COPY the files that have matching msf entries (but NOT the msf entries), such as inbox, sent, etc
  8. PASTE into the Mail\LocalFolders folder (you get to the Mail folder by clicking back to where you were at step 5 and there the Mail folder will appear)
  9. restart thunderbird and check that the messages are in the Local Folders folder. Now, when you remove the imap account, you will still have the messages.

content:
Where did the address file that you imported into Thunderbird come from, and what format is it? I'm guessing the file is in CSV (Comma-Separated Values) format. Thunderbird needs the fields to be in a certain order to import it properly. If the file was created by another program, the fields may not be in the right order. Usually, if Thunderbird detects that your import file isn't right, it will open a window that lets you defined the fields to its liking. Did you see such a window (see attached image)? This can be a very tedious process that can be made easier by importing the CSV into a spreadsheet program and re-arranging the column order to agree with Thunderbird's preference. Below is a template for Thunderbird:

First Name,Last Name,Display Name,Nickname,Primary Email,Secondary Email,Screen Name,Work Phone,Home Phone,Fax Number,Pager Number,Mobile Number,Home Address,Home Address 2,Home City,Home State,Home ZipCode,Home Country,Work Address,Work Address 2,Work City,Work State,Work ZipCode,Work Country,Job Title,Department,Organization,Web Page 1,Web Page 2,Birth Year,Birth Month,Birth Day,Custom 1,Custom 2,Custom 3,Custom 4,Notes

The easiest solution would be if the file to import is in VCF (vCard) format, in which the fields are standardized. So, if possible, go back to the origin of your address book and export it in vCard format.

content:
hello there

It seems that even after removing an account, Thunderbird may keep old configuration data and saved credentials. Deleting these manually might resolve the issue without needing a full reinstallation. Please follow these steps: Open Thunderbird Settings. Go to Privacy & Security. Scroll down to Passwords and click Saved Passwords. Click Show Passwords. Search for "posteo" and "web.de". If you find any related entries, delete them. Thunderbird often stores authentication data in a separate secure vault, which is why these settings can remain even after an account is deleted.
creator: Googlethunderbird.

content:
hello there


taking the deepclean 

    via config.editor

Deep cleaning via the Config Editor Open Thunderbird and go to Settings. On the General tab, scroll all the way to the bottom and click the Config Editor button. In the search bar at the top, type: calendar.registry You will see a list of rows. Look for entries containing posteo.de or web.de and click the trash/delete icon next to them. Next, clear the search bar and type: print_printer If you see any entries related to your old account, delete or reset those as well. Restart Thunderbird.

This does not work on mobile devices because the text and data are different from the desktop version.

mobile devices Most of these subjects focus on cleaning parts of the software that could become too full of data. ios Primary Fix: The problem is almost always located in Settings > Mail > Accounts. Locate the old account and select Delete Account.

content:
Is this still an accurate summary? Caldav and (or) cardav authentication prompts related to https://posteo.de


Make sure you don't have multiple prefs.js files.

Did you ever previously use postbox or betterbird?

SUMO indicates you are on macOS 10.15. Is that correct?

Do you have the same issue on a different newer device?

I wouldn't trust calendars that are deactivated.

  • backup your prefs.js
  • look in for prefs.js for all lines with oauth2.scope and make sure none reference calendar

And if you don't have any local calendars that you need to save - clean house as follows, then add back any calendars that you need:

  • delete everything in prefs.js with lines user_pref("calendar.
  • delete calendar-data directory from the thunderbird profile
